commit_message: good progress on front end, organized the js files a bit, password and memory reset implemented

user_prompt: during password reset through the web interface, I get `N.Pbkdf2HmacSha256 is not a function` as the error, something is wrong with the webcrypt shim?
agent_reply: Yep, that was the shim. Its PBKDF2 path tries to call `asmCrypto.Pbkdf2HmacSha256`, but the bundled shim does not actually provide that function, so password reset dies before it can POST the new key.

I fixed [sec_helpers.js](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/sec_helpers.js:33) so `derive_pbkdf2_bytes()` still tries WebCrypto first, but falls back to a local JS PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 implementation if the shim/native path fails. I also rebuilt the packaged web asset in [web_assets.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h:3).
Verified the fallback against Node’s `crypto.pbkdf2Sync` for known vectors, then built:
Both passed. Sec0 had one transient stale `.pio` object failure on the first try, then passed cleanly on retry.

commit_message: fixed encrypted recording being too short

user_prompt: ok hold on... you say network-key, I actually did write down that I wanted the session-key to be used for the encryption
I see a `sessionAesKey` in the javascript but I don't see it used, and I see a `uint8_t  session_key[kSessionKeySize] = {};` in the `WebServer.h` code
I am reading `.codex_history\lib\WifiManager\WebServer.h.codexhist.json`, it says " * encrypt the gigantic string using session-key" in my instructions
if my request is asking for something less secure than what you've actually implemented, you need to justify it to me, otherwise, I want minimum usage of the network-key during Wi-Fi sessions
the network-key as a bigger role in cloud related work
agent_reply: You were right. I don’t think there’s a good justification for using the network key as the Wi-Fi-session transport encryption key here. It had drifted away from your original “encrypt with session-key” intent, so I changed it.
- Browser derives `network_key` from password only long enough to prove login and derive `session_key`.
- Browser no longer stores `networkHmacKey` or `networkAesKey` in `session_security`.
- `/set_cfg`, `/set_custom_pin`, and `/reset_pin_code` bodies are encrypted with the session key.
- `/set_cfg` now sends the session auth headers too.
- Backend authenticates those headers with `authenticateSessionRequest(...)`, derives `session_key`, and decrypts the request body with that session key.
- PIN reset/update uses the same session-key decrypt path.
- Client-to-server POST nonces now force a separate nonce domain from server-to-client `/get_cfg` responses.
So the corrected documentation line is:

> For security levels >= 1, config transport is session-authenticated and session-encrypted. The network key is used to prove the password-derived credential and derive the per-session key; the session key encrypts `/get_cfg` responses and `/set_cfg`/PIN POST bodies.

Verified with `node --check` for both JS files, `pio run -e thefly_sec0/sec1/sec2/sec3` after the C++ change, and a final `pio run -e thefly_sec1` after the last web-asset tweak. `git diff --check` is clean aside from line-ending warnings.

user_prompt: ### ask: decrypted download

You’ve helped me benchmark how fast it takes to decrypt a file on-device, it was slow, like a one hour meeting recording would take maybe 20 minutes to decrypt, but, that was mostly because of the microSD card simultaneous reading and writing being a bottleneck.
I want to implement decrypt while downloading. Hopefully this is faster.

The framework for authentication and encrypted communication is already established in the front-end. We’ve also added a unlock button for files that are `*.rec`, right now clicking on them does nothing. This button is shown and hidden appropriately for the given file name and login state.
So in `lib\WifiManager\WebFileHandlers.cpp` we will add a handler for `/decrypted_download`, registered in `lib\WifiManager\WebServer.cpp`

The front-end will construct a JSON object, with “file_name” as a key to indicate which file is being requested, along with a key “action” indicating “download”. This JSON blob is encrypted with the `sessionAesKey` before being sent to `/decrypted_download`. The CPP side of `/decrypted_download` will decrypt it using `uint8_t session_key[]` , failure to decrypt would indicate not being authenticated
Also handle when “action” is “stream”, then the mime-type is one that indicates that it is a `*.wav` audio file. If the key “action” is missing, then use mime-type being octet stream.

If authenticated and the file exists, then stream it back to the client decrypted chunk by decrypted chunk. The indicated download file name should have a `*.wav` extension. The mime-type should be octet stream
I believe as we have known sizes for chunks both encrypted and decrypted, estimating the unencrypted file size should be easy
agent_reply: The unlock button now posts an encrypted session-key request to `/decrypted_download` from [html/script.js](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:267), then saves the decrypted response as a `.wav`.

- Added encrypted request parsing plus chunk-by-chunk `.rec` to WAV streaming in [WebFileHandlers.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:458) and [WebFileHandlers.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:1293)
- Request JSON uses `file_name` plus `action: "download"`, encrypted with `sessionAesKey`
- Server authenticates session headers, decrypts with `session_key`
- GCM decrypt failure returns auth-style failure
- WAV size is estimated from encrypted chunk count and the decrypted WAV header is patched accordingly
- `git diff --check` clean except normal CRLF warnings
Generated files changed from the builds: `inc/web_assets.h` and `src/version.c`.

user_prompt: uhhhh 10000 rounds took 5 seconds...
yea we lowering this to like 100 lol
agent_reply: Done. Session-key PBKDF2 is now `100` rounds on both sides, while password-derived keys stay at `100000`.
- server `/get_cfg` session key derivation now uses that session-specific constant
- `git diff --check` passes, with only CRLF warnings
That should turn the session derivation from “oh no” to basically instant, while keeping the expensive KDF where it actually matters: password to key.
Done. Session-key PBKDF2 is now `100` rounds on both sides, while password-derived keys stay at `100000`.
- server `/get_cfg` session key derivation now uses that session-specific constant
- `git diff --check` passes, with only CRLF warnings
That should turn the session derivation from “oh no” to basically instant, while keeping the expensive KDF where it actually matters: password to key.

user_prompt: implement this, both front-end and back-end
agent_reply: Implemented the GET-based decrypted download flow.
- Frontend now encrypts the filename/action payload, base64url-encodes it, and navigates to:
- Browser should now treat the decrypted WAV as a normal download immediately, using the server’s `Content-Disposition`.
- Backend added `GET /decrypted_download` alongside the old POST path.
- Backend decodes the base64url request, authenticates using query params as fallback for the usual session headers, decrypts the request, then streams the WAV.
Implemented the GET-based decrypted download flow.
- Frontend now encrypts the filename/action payload, base64url-encodes it, and navigates to:
- Browser should now treat the decrypted WAV as a normal download immediately, using the server’s `Content-Disposition`.
- Backend added `GET /decrypted_download` alongside the old POST path.
- Backend decodes the base64url request, authenticates using query params as fallback for the usual session headers, decrypts the request, then streams the WAV.

commit_message: file download and decrypted downloads work now

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: html/sec_helpers.js
## Active selection of the file:
pbkdf
## My request for Codex:
implement `function benchmark_key_derivation()` inside `html\sec_helpers.js`
all I need it to do is perform pbkdf key derivation, with different number of iterations, and measure how long it takes
start with 1 iteration, then 5, then 10, then 50, then 100, then 500, 1000, 5000, 10000, 50000, 100000, then end the test
use console.log to show the results
the function will be called from the developer tools console
make sure it is using the shim functions, not native webcrypto even if it is available
agent_reply: It now runs PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-256 at `1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500, 1000, 5000, 10000, 50000, 100000` iterations, logs elapsed milliseconds for each, and deliberately calls the local JavaScript shim/fallback PBKDF2 function directly rather than native WebCrypto.
Verified with the bundled Node syntax check: `node --check html\sec_helpers.js`.

## My request for Codex:

although sometimes it does get a 200 code and the page title but not the rest of the page

agent_reply: I changed the page load path to stop blocking on the huge WebCrypto shim.

In [html/index.html](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/index.html:5), I removed the synchronous `<script src="webcrypto-shim.min.js">` from the head. `sec_helpers.js` already lazy-loads that file when crypto is actually needed, so the fallback remains available.

In [tools/package_web_assets.py](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/tools/package_web_assets.py:52), I made `webcrypto-shim.min.js` a deferred standalone packaged asset, then regenerated [inc/web_assets.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h:6).
- `webcrypto-shim.min.js` is now served separately at `129520` compressed bytes
I left the Wi-Fi/AP start behavior alone. I also didn’t need `sterile_rebuild.py` here because the normal build clearly regenerated assets and completed cleanly.
